papers.title: Recoil geometry determines electromagnetic counterparts from supermassive black hole merger remnants
summary_bullets: This paper presents the first general-relativistic magnetohydrodynamic (GRMHD) simulations of a recoiling supermassive black hole (SMBH) interacting with a magnetically arrested circumbinary disk that was consistently evolved through the inspiral; simulations explore recoil speeds (v_r up to 0.10c) and three recoil geometries (vertical, oblique, horizontal).

They demonstrate qualitatively distinct post-merger outcomes tied to recoil geometry: perpendicular recoils leave a bound inner disk and sustain relativistic jets, in-plane (horizontal) recoils drive strong bow shocks, copious shock heating and eventual jet quenching, while oblique recoils tilt the accretion flow and produce intermittent jet–disk interactions and episodic outbursts. The simulations also identify MAD-specific features (magnetic flux tubes, reconnection sites) that can power nonthermal flares and quantify thermal energy injection in the disk as a function of recoil path.

Key words/topics: GRMHD simulations, magnetically arrested disk (MAD), SMBH mergers, gravitational-wave recoil kicks, jet launching and quenching, shock heating, magnetic reconnection, EM afterglows, LISA-era multi-messenger counterparts; posted on arXiv 27 Mar 2026 (very recent).

papers.title: An Online Approach for Entanglement Verification Using Classical Shadows
summary_bullets: The paper develops an online hybrid quantum–classical framework that processes classical shadows incrementally during the slack time between measurement shots, enabling real-time updates rather than offline post-processing.

Focusing on entanglement verification via PT-moments (trace moments of the partially transposed density matrix), the authors derive two unbiased online estimators that characterize the trade-off between memory footprint and per-shot computational cost: one memory-efficient estimator works directly from raw measurement records without constructing empirical density matrices, while the other maintains accumulated matrices to achieve constant per-shot cost and handle high moment orders.

By exploiting all combinations of snapshots and the incremental structure of classical shadows, the online estimators require fewer samples than state-of-the-art baselines in relevant regimes and turn PT-moment based entanglement detection from a purely offline diagnostic into a protocol that runs concurrently with experiments.

papers.title: The High-Mass-Ratio Challenge in Gravitational Waveform Modelling
summary_bullets: The paper presents new numerical-relativity simulations of highly asymmetric, strongly precessing binary black holes with mass ratio q = 18 and primary spin χ1 = 0.8 (secondary non-spinning), exploring five spin-misalignment angles (30°, 60°, 90°, 120°, 150°).

Comparing these NR waveforms to current state-of-the-art waveform models (IMRPhenomXPNR, IMRPhenomTPHM, SEOBNRv5PHM, NRSur7dq4) the authors find systematically large mismatches (≳0.1) across much of this regime. Targeted parameter-estimation injections into a three-detector LIGO–Virgo network reveal dramatic parameter biases in some cases (mass errors >100%), showing an urgent need for NR-calibrated improvements and offering these simulations as calibration data.
This is directly relevant to waveform-modeling and inference systematics for high-q, precessing BBHs and supplies scarce long, accurate NR waveforms in that corner of parameter space.

papers.title: Simulation-based Inference towards Gravitational-wave waveform systematics in Intermediate-Mass Binary Black Holes
summary_bullets: Using a large simulated training set drawn equally from IMRPhenomXPHM and SEOBNRv5PHM, the authors train a neural posterior estimator based on neural-spline normalising flows with a jointly learned dual-domain (frequency + time) embedding to amortise parameter estimation for intermediate-mass binary black hole (IMBH) signals. By treating the waveform-model index as a latent variable the network learns posteriors marginalised over waveform-model systematics and returns samples in milliseconds, demonstrating close agreement with evidence-weighted bilby-dynesty results while reducing inference time by orders of magnitude. The approach is validated on Gaussian-noise injections, recovers a 13-dimensional subset of a 15-D training prior, and enables large-scale calibration tests (10^4 injections) thanks to the millisecond inference cost.

This paper directly addresses waveform-systematics and rapid, systematics-aware inference for high-mass BBH signals—topics tightly connected to Yanbei's interests in waveform modeling, strong-field tests, and black-hole astrophysics; marginalising over approximants (IMRPhenomXPHM vs SEOBNRv5PHM) is especially relevant for extracting robust strong-gravity observables from short, few-cycle IMBH mergers. The dual-domain embedding and the prospect of extending the training set to include NR surrogates, non-Gaussian noise, eccentricity or additional dynamical effects make the method promising for Yanbei's work on improving waveform-systematics, performing population studies, and enabling fast follow-up tests of GR in regimes where perturbation- and nonlinear effects matter.

Keywords/topics: Simulation-based inference (SBI), Neural Posterior Estimation (NPE), normalising flows (neural spline flows), waveform systematics, IMBHs, IMRPhenomXPHM, SEOBNRv5PHM, dual-domain (time+frequency) embedding, amortised inference, LIGO LVK pipelines. Publication/recency: arXiv:2603.26405 (gr-qc), v1 posted 27–30 March 2026 (very recent).

papers.title: Majorana-XYZ subsystem code
summary_bullets: This paper studies Majorana-XYZ subsystem code using the methods and problem setup described in the abstract. Its scientific content appears to center on quant-ph themes highlighted by the metadata. The apparent novelty is inferred only from the abstract/title metadata because PDF summarization was unavailable.
Yanbei may care because it connects to his previous work and current interests, especially quantum noise in interferometers, quantum measurement theory; and also to quantum error correction.
Key words/topics: quantum error correction; published about 3.2 days ago.

papers.title: Noise modelling of waveguide based squeezed light sources
summary_bullets: This paper presents a comprehensive noise model for waveguide-based optical parametric amplifiers that generate squeezed light, deriving coupled‑mode and matrix formalisms to quantify the effects of phase noise, in-/out‑coupling, propagation and detection losses, and unconverted fundamental leakage (using the PDF excerpt results). It shows that phase noise and distributed losses determine an optimal squeezing parameter and that ppLN waveguides—owing to high nonlinearity, THz bandwidth, and intrinsically low phase noise—can approach cavity squeezer performance while being primarily limited by out‑coupling and fabrication‑induced propagation loss. The authors further model and propose a cascaded (phase‑sensitive) waveguide OPA (SU(1,1) style) to mitigate detection and out‑coupling losses, giving tradeoffs and design criteria aimed at applications in gravitational‑wave detectors such as the Einstein Telescope.

Why Yanbei would care: the paper addresses quantum‑noise engineering for interferometric precision measurement—directly relevant to Yanbei’s work on quantum noise in interferometers, quantum measurement theory, back‑action evasion and optomechanics—by providing a practically realistic route (integrated, low‑phase‑noise waveguide squeezers and cascaded amplification) to reduce quantum noise and backscatter problems in GW detectors; additionally, the high bandwidth and integrability make these sources attractive for tabletop optomechanical tests and quantum‑limited sensors that probe macroscopic quantum behavior or gravity‑related decoherence/entanglement scenarios that Yanbei is currently interested in.

Keywords/topics: squeezed light, waveguide OPA (ppLN/ppLNOI), phase noise/phase jitter, in‑/out‑coupling and propagation loss, leakage/seeding, cascaded phase‑sensitive amplification (SU(1,1)), noise budgeting, applications to gravitational‑wave detectors and integrated photonics. Publication: arXiv:2603.26243 (submitted 27 Mar 2026) — very recent.

papers.title: Minimal noise in non-quantized gravity
summary_bullets: The authors provide a general parametrization of all non-quantized (non-graviton) models of Newtonian-limit gravity under minimal assumptions (Galilean invariance, time locality, and Ehrenfest-averaged Newtonian forces), showing the dissipative part of the dynamics can be written as Lindblad operators L_{k,i}=e^{ik·R}J_{k,i}(r) and reduced to single-body and correlated two-body noise functions. They prove that any model that fails to entangle two masses must inject a quantifiable, irreducible minimum amount of noise, derive explicit noise/entanglement thresholds for several experimental setups (mechanical oscillators, two-position-state masses, hybrid source–two-state systems), and give concrete numerical benchmarks (e.g., acceleration-noise threshold ~10^{-18} m/s^2/√Hz at 1 mm for equal masses). The framework subsumes prior classical-quantum and entropic-gravity proposals, keeps non-Gaussian single-body dissipation terms (avoiding Gaussian truncations used previously), and supplies Lindblad equations and entanglement/noise criteria directly applicable to tabletop force-sensing experiments.

Yanbei should care because the paper translates alternative gravity hypotheses into experimentally measurable noise budgets and explicit Lindblad dynamics that tie directly to quantum-noise, back-action, and force-sensing techniques he has developed in interferometry and optomechanics; the derived force-noise thresholds and the role of correlated versus local noise give concrete targets and modelling prescriptions for proposals to detect gravity-induced entanglement or rule out non-quantized gravity. The emphasis on non-Gaussian single-body dissipation and on mapping concrete CQ/entropic models into parameter space will inform how to design, analyze, and interpret tabletop experiments (including readout and error budgets) relevant to his interests in gravity-induced decoherence/entanglement and quantum gravity phenomenology.

Keywords/topics: non-quantized gravity, Lindblad master equation, gravitational decoherence, gravitationally induced noise, Newtonian entanglement, force-noise thresholds, classical-quantum models, entropic gravity, optomechanics, tabletop quantum-gravity tests; Posted on arXiv (v1) 27 Mar 2026 — a very recent work.

papers.title: Detectability and Systematic Bias from First-Order Phase-Transition Dephasing in Kerr EMRIs
summary_bullets: The paper builds a controlled phenomenological model of a first-order phase transition in the dissipative flux sector of a circular equatorial Kerr EMRI, implemented as a finite-width restructuring of an effective coupling that perturbs the flux and therefore the inspiral clock.

Using this model and standard LISA matched-filter diagnostics the authors show that a narrow transition window can produce very large accumulated dephasing (benchmark: ΔΦ22 ≃ 5×10^3 rad) while yielding a deceptively small normalized mismatch (M ≃ 2.986×10^−3 after maximization over time and phase) and a detector-weighted residual norm of order unity.

The novel and central result is an explicit identification of a bias-sensitive regime in which detectability is preserved but waveform faithfulness and precision parameter inference are compromised, motivating the incorporation of parameterized transition sectors into high-accuracy EMRI waveform models.

papers.title: Binary neutron star mergers with tabulated equations of state in SPHINCS_BSSN
summary_bullets: Using the Lagrangian numerical-relativity code SPHINCS_BSSN, the authors implement conservative-to-primitive (con2prim) recovery for tabulated high-density equations of state and present three algorithms: a 3D Newton–Raphson solver, a 2D Newton–Raphson variant, and a 1D Ridders' root-finder. They demonstrate in a full binary neutron-star merger with the DD2 table that the 3D scheme is very fast and robust (average failure fraction well below 1%), while the 1D Ridders' method, although much slower, serves as a practically fail-safe fallback. The paper supplies detailed algebraic reductions, Jacobian formulae, convergence diagnostics and a production strategy (3D NR default, 1D Ridders' parachute) specifically adapted to SPHINCS_BSSN's evolved conservative variables and tabulated-EOS interpolation.

Why Yanbei would care: the work directly improves the robustness and physical fidelity of numerical-relativity simulations that produce inspiral, merger and post-merger gravitational-wave signals sensitive to the high-density EOS and nonlinear strong-field dynamics—areas relevant to Yanbei's interests in waveform modeling, strong-gravity tests tied to observables, and nonlinear GW interactions. A reliable con2prim pipeline for tabulated EOS reduces spurious failures and bias in post-merger dynamics (oscillation spectra, damping/tails, possible nonlinear mode couplings) and thus makes simulation outputs more trustworthy for EOS inference, tests of GR with post-merger features, and for building or validating data-analysis templates for next-generation detectors. Additionally, SPHINCS_BSSN's hybrid particle+AMR approach and the provided fallback strategy could be of interest when comparing different NR methods' systematics or when seeking robust waveform inputs for studies of GW memory, nonlinear mode excitation, or matter–gravity couplings.

Keywords/topics: SPHINCS_BSSN; conservative-to-primitive inversion; tabulated equation of state; 3D/2D Newton–Raphson; Ridders' method; smoothed-particle hydrodynamics; BSSN spacetime evolution; binary neutron-star mergers; post-merger oscillations and waveform modeling; numerical-relativity algorithms. Published/submitted: arXiv:2603.25809 (submitted 26 Mar 2026; PDF draft dated 30 Mar 2026).

papers.title: Ultrabroadband Passive Laser Noise Suppression to Quantum Noise Limit through on-chip Second Harmonic Generation
summary_bullets: This paper demonstrates a passive, all‑optical "photonic integrated nonlinear noise eater" (PINE) that uses highly efficient on‑chip second‑harmonic generation in thin‑film lithium niobate to suppress laser intensity noise from DC to >10 GHz. Operating at a pump‑depletion stationary point (≈70% conversion) decouples first‑order input fluctuations from the transmitted fundamental, yielding intrinsic >25–60 dB relative intensity noise (RIN) suppression and stabilization of a noisy fiber amplifier to the shot‑noise limit. Because the device is single‑pass, non‑resonant, compact, and broadband (bandwidth set by group‑velocity walk‑off rather than electronic feedback), it provides a scalable chip‑scale route to ultra‑low‑RIN light and potentially on‑chip amplitude‑squeezed sources for quantum metrology.

Yanbei should care because this technique directly tackles classical intensity noise and produces shot‑noise‑limited (and potentially squeezed) optical carriers that are central to his work on quantum noise in interferometers, quantum measurement theory, QND/back‑action evasion and optomechanics; a passive, ultrabroadband, chip‑scale low‑RIN source would reduce technical noise floors in precision interferometry, atom/ion control, and tabletop tests of gravity‑induced decoherence or entanglement. The elimination of electronic feedback bandwidth limits and the compatibility with integrated photonics make the PINE immediately relevant for deploying low‑noise local oscillators and squeezed‑light generation in experiments Yanbei and collaborators design and analyze.

Keywords/topics: second‑harmonic generation (χ(2)), thin‑film lithium niobate (TFLN), passive noise eater, relative intensity noise (RIN), shot‑noise limit, ultrabroadband (>10 GHz), integrated photonics, squeezed light, laser stabilization, quantum metrology, optomechanics, atom/ion control. Published: arXiv v1 26 March 2026 (very recent).

papers.title: Primordial Black Hole interpretation of the sub-solar merger event S251112cm
summary_bullets: The paper assesses whether the LVK O4 candidate S251112cm, which shows a chirp mass implying at least one sub-solar component, can be explained as a merger of primordial black holes (PBHs) with masses in the 0.1–1 M⊙ range by combining analytic PBH binary merger-rate estimates with LVK detector sensitivity and observational constraints. The authors compute merger rates for equal-mass, monochromatic PBH binaries, calibrate an effective spacetime volume ⟨VT⟩ from O3/O4a injection results with an empirical scaling ⟨VT⟩∝M^2.34 for sub-solar masses, and use Poisson statistics to obtain the probability P(MPBH) of observing ≥1 event as a function of PBH mass and abundance. The novel quantitative result is that, under relaxed microlensing constraints and extrapolated O4a sensitivity, the detection probability can approach unity for MPBH≈0.5–1 M⊙ (and remains sizable ≲O(0.5) in conservative cases), showing sub-solar GW candidates are a viable probe of PBHs and their dark-matter fraction.

Yanbei would care because this paper directly connects population-level PBH merger-rate theory and detector sensitivity to a concrete LVK candidate, tying early-Universe models and dark-matter hypotheses to observable GW statistics; this complements his interests in black-hole physics and strong-gravity tests by highlighting a clean sub-solar mass window largely free of stellar backgrounds. The work also raises practical modelling questions (e.g., template sensitivity, mass-function assumptions, and selection effects) that intersect with his expertise in waveform modeling, EMRI/perturbation techniques and tests of GR, and it motivates targeted searches and theoretical work to distinguish primordial from exotic astrophysical compact objects.

Keywords/topics: primordial black holes, sub-solar mass mergers, LVK O3/O4a sensitivity scaling, PBH merger rates, microlensing constraints, detector selection effects, dark matter probes. Posted to arXiv March 26, 2026 (PDF dated March 30, 2026) — very recent.

papers.title: Negative energies and the breakdown of bulk geometry
summary_bullets: The authors compute inner products of fixed-length (|ℓ⟩) states in pure 2D JT gravity by summing the gravitational path integral over all topologies and matching to the dual random-matrix boundary description. They show that nonperturbative contributions from rare ensemble members with negative-energy eigenstates produce exponentially large corrections that cause the semiclassical bulk Hilbert space to break down already at geodesic lengths ℓ ∼ e^{S0/3}, parametrically shorter than the previously expected ℓ ∼ e^{S0}, with both the mean and variance scaling like exp[const·(¯ℓ e^{-S0/3})^{3/2}]. The mechanism is tied to the universal Airy-regime density of states and instanton effects in the matrix model and has striking consequences such as an infinite expectation value for a natural definition of the wormhole length in the thermofield-double state.

You would care because this paper identifies a new, nonperturbative route (negative-energy rare states in the dual matrix ensemble) by which semiclassical geometry and naive bulk-to-boundary maps fail at much shorter scales than expected; that changes the regime where quantum fluctuations dominate geometric observables — precisely the sort of semiclassical-to-quantum transition relevant to your interests in gravity-induced decoherence/entanglement, the foundations of quantum measurement in gravitational settings, and how microscopic corrections affect observable quantities (e.g. length growth of wormholes). The techniques (topology resummation in JT gravity, Airy-kernel universality, and matrix-model instantons) and the emphasis on concrete diagnostics of breakdowns of effective descriptions are directly relevant to framing phenomenological or information-theoretic tests of quantum gravity and to thinking about error-correction/encoding of bulk geometry into boundary degrees of freedom.

Keywords/topics: JT gravity, random-matrix dual, nonperturbative effects, negative-energy states, Airy regime, genus/topology resummation, wormhole length, thermofield double, semiclassical breakdown. Published: arXiv v1 posted 2026-03-26 (very recent).

papers.title: Repetitive Penrose Process in Kerr-Taub-NUT black hole spacetime
summary_bullets: The paper analyzes the repetitive Penrose process in the Kerr–Taub–NUT spacetime, deriving horizon and ergosphere loci and showing that both radii increase with the gravitomagnetic (NUT) charge l while the irreducible mass behavior causes the maximum extractable rotational energy to decrease as l grows. The authors formulate iterative conservation laws and stopping conditions for repeated particle-splitting cycles, give analytic expressions for turning points and relevant orbital radii (marginally bound and photon orbits) in the Kerr–Taub–NUT metric, and track how M, a, irreducible mass and extractable energy evolve per iteration. Numerical examples (̂l = 0.2 with rp = 1.4 and 1.6) show the process terminates after a finite number of cycles, quantify energy-return and utilization efficiencies, and demonstrate that a fraction of the extractable energy is converted into irreducible mass rather than being fully liberated.

Why Yanbei would care: the paper connects horizon/ergosphere geometry, spin and irreducible-mass evolution under repeated energy-extraction—topics directly related to Yanbei’s interests in black-hole physics, event-horizon properties and strong-gravity observables. Although Kerr–Taub–NUT is an exotic (Misner-string) extension, the explicit analytic relations and numeric tracking of how non-Kerr gravitomagnetic charge modifies energetics and stopping conditions could inform work on spin evolution, bounds on rotational-energy reservoirs (relevant to jet physics), and hypothesis tests where non-Kerr multipoles or topology change observables such as emitted radiation or mode frequencies in perturbed black holes. The iterative, nonlinear character of the process and its bookkeeping of irreducible mass make it potentially useful background for thinking about backreaction in EMRIs or nonlinear oscillation scenarios Yanbei studies.

Keywords/topics: Kerr–Taub–NUT spacetime, NUT/gravitomagnetic charge, repetitive Penrose process, ergosphere and horizon structure, irreducible mass, marginally bound / photon orbits, iterative stopping conditions, energy-extraction efficiency, numerical examples. Published: 26 March 2026 (arXiv:2603.25630) — very recent.

papers.title: Particle Physics and Gravitational Waves as complementary windows on the Universe
summary_bullets: This Perspectives article surveys how gravitational-wave observations—from neutron-star tidal signals and black-hole binaries to stochastic backgrounds measurable by LISA and third-generation detectors—can probe particle-physics phenomena such as the QCD phase diagram, first-order electroweak‑scale phase transitions, and dark-matter scenarios including primordial black holes and ultralight bosons.

The paper highlights concrete observational signatures and detectors: NS inspiral/merger constraints on dense QCD, stochastic GW backgrounds from TeV‑scale phase transitions accessible to LISA, environment‑induced waveform dephasing from dark‑matter spikes and ‘gravitational‑atom’ effects due to superradiance, and synergies with collider, direct‑detection and atom‑interferometer programs.

Its novelty is synthesising particle‑physics targets and GW observables across frequency bands (pulsar‑timing, LISA, ET/CE, atom interferometers), mapping which experiments probe which particle models, and advocating a coordinated multi‑messenger program to maximise discovery potential in the 2030s–2040s.

papers.title: Implementing Bell causality in Quantum Sequential Growth
summary_bullets: The authors analyse different operator-ordering implementations of quantum Bell causality (QBC) within the quantum sequential growth (QSG) framework of causal-set quantum gravity, allowing non-commuting transition operators and assuming non-singular dynamics. They prove that for the two most natural orderings the transition-operator algebra A reduces to a commutative algebra (recovering the complex sequential growth, CSG, models), while a third ordering that depends on the size of the precursor set yields new commutation relations that do not force commutativity. They also show that if any generator of the antichain subalgebra Q lies in its center then A becomes commutative, and that a simple d=2 Pauli-matrix representation of Q is inconsistent, implying any non-trivial representation must be higher-dimensional—positioning this work as a first step toward a genuinely non-commutative QSG.

You would care because the paper probes how Bell-causality constraints and operator-ordering ambiguities shape the algebraic (commutative versus non-commutative) structure of quantum-gravitational transition operators—a question closely related to the foundations of quantum measurement and how gravity might mediate or constrain entanglement and decoherence. Given your background on quantum measurement theory, back-action, and optomechanics, the result that non-commutative realizations are nontrivial (and likely high-dimensional) bears on modelling effective quantum channels or noise induced by Planck-scale/causal-set degrees of freedom and on whether such channels could produce observable gravity-induced decoherence or entanglement signatures. While the work is foundational and formal rather than directly experimental, its algebraic constraints could inform phenomenological models used to predict signatures in tabletop tests or interferometric probes of quantum gravity.

Keywords/topics: causal set theory, quantum sequential growth (QSG), quantum Bell causality (QBC), operator ordering, non-commutative operator algebra, antichain subalgebra, decoherence functional, quantum gravity foundations. Publication: arXiv:2603.25503, posted 26 March 2026 (very recent).

papers.title: Testing the strong equivalence principle with multimessenger binary neutron star mergers
summary_bullets: The authors construct a gravitational-wave waveform model for a slowly time-varying Newton constant G that self-consistently includes both source-generation effects (object-dependent sensitivities entering inspiral phasing and amplitude) and propagation effects across an FLRW background, implementing ppE-like corrections (a = −8, b = −13) and enforcing EOS-dependent neutron-star mass/sensitivity bounds via TOV solutions. They compute neutron-star sensitivities numerically for multiple EOSs, give a model-independent argument that stationary black-hole sensitivity = 1/2, and perform full Bayesian inference on GW170817 combining GW data with electromagnetic priors (GRB 170817A) using TaylorF2 and IMRPhenomXAS_NRTidalv2 waveforms. The multimessenger analysis yields no evidence for ˙G and produces a conservative 3σ bound ˙G/G ∈ [−3.36×10^−9, 5.34×10^−10] yr^−1, claimed as the most stringent constraint from real GW observations to date.

This paper should interest you because it provides a concrete, data-ready prescription for incorporating a fundamental-constant variation into waveform templates (phase and amplitude) and demonstrates how multimessenger priors break degeneracies — directly relevant to your work on waveform modeling, strong-field tests of GR, and GW data-analysis/statistical inference. The use of EOS-dependent TOV sensitivities and the explicit derivation of black-hole sensitivity = 1/2 connect to your interests in compact-object physics and horizon properties, while the propagation-side modifications bear on cosmological/modified-gravity probes (dark energy/cosmic evolution) that you follow.

Keywords/topics: varying G, strong equivalence principle, multimessenger binary neutron star, GW170817, ppE corrections (a = −8, b = −13), neutron-star sensitivities (TOV), black-hole sensitivity = 1/2, Bayesian parameter inference, waveform systematics. Published: arXiv:2603.25413 (26 Mar 2026).

papers.title: Catalytic Coherence Amplification for Quantum State Recovery: Theory, Numerical Validation, and Comparison with Conventional Error Correction
summary_bullets: Catalytic Quantum Error Correction (CQEC) is a state-recovery protocol that uses catalytic covariant (energy‑conserving) transformations to arbitrarily amplify coherence: if the coherent modes of a known target state are contained within those of multiple noisy copies (mode inclusion), a reusable catalyst mediates a covariant channel that restores the target fidelity without an error threshold. The authors construct a variational energy‑conserving circuit ansatz, derive finite‑copy scaling (1−F ≤ O(1/√n)), and numerically validate CQEC across four quantum algorithms and a tree tensor‑network cryptographic protocol under dephasing, depolarizing and combined noise, showing asymptotic recovery from F ≈ 0.07 to F > 0.999. They compare CQEC to Steane and surface codes, demonstrate 100‑cycle catalyst reuse with negligible deviation, and identify practical limitations (copy overhead, exponential catalyst dimension) and the regimes where coherence‑based recovery outperforms conventional QEC.

Why Yanbei would care: CQEC frames error protection in the language of coherence resource theory and covariant (energy‑conserving) operations, directly connecting to his expertise in quantum noise, quantum measurement theory, back‑action evasion and optomechanics where energy conservation and phase/coherence are central; the paper explicitly uses quantum Fisher information and mode structure to quantify recoverable metrological resources. The protocol’s threshold‑free recovery and correlated catalyst reuse suggest new, lower‑overhead strategies for restoring fragile macroscopic or metrological quantum states (potentially relevant for tabletop tests of gravity‑induced decoherence or gravity‑mediated entanglement), while its requirements—known target state, many noisy copies and large catalyst Hilbert space—map onto realistic experimental tradeoffs Yanbei studies.

Keywords/topics: catalytic quantum error correction, coherence resource theory, covariant/energy‑conserving operations, coherent modes/mode inclusion, correlated catalysis, quantum Fisher information, variational EC circuits, comparison with Steane/surface codes; arXiv:2603.25774 (dated March 2026 — very recent).

papers.title: Non parametric constraints of gravitational-electromagnetic luminosity distance ratio
summary_bullets: The authors develop a non-parametric reconstruction of the gravitational-to-electromagnetic luminosity-distance ratio r(z) using a Piecewise Cubic Hermite Interpolating Polynomial (PCHIP) and implement it into the gwcosmo hierarchical Bayesian pipeline.

They apply this method to a joint population–cosmology analysis of 42 binary-black-hole dark-siren events from GWTC‑3, combining sky-localization, galaxy-catalogue priors, and detector-frame mass information.

The results are consistent with general relativity and previous parametric constraints, and the approach is designed to map directly onto effective gravitational-coupling / modified-gravity parameters without assuming a specific theoretical parametrization.

papers.title: Magnetic Modification of Black Hole Photospheres with Image Contraction, Efficiency Shifts and Redshift Boosts in Schwarzschild-Bertotti-Robinson Spacetime
summary_bullets: Using the exact Schwarzschild–Bertotti–Robinson solution to the Einstein–Maxwell equations, the authors study null geodesics and Novikov–Thorne thin-disk radiation to show that a self-consistent uniform magnetic field alters photon propagation and expands light bundles relative to Schwarzschild.

They compute magnetic-field-dependent shifts of the event horizon, photon sphere and ISCO (all moving outward with increasing B), produce ray-traced disk images showing contraction of the direct image with enhanced maximal energy flux, temperature and redshift, and derive analytic expressions for the modified Keplerian frequency, specific energy and angular momentum including an exact ISCO; for β = B M ≃ 0.1 they report an ≃91% drop in radiative efficiency.
The work highlights potential observable discriminants of magnetic fields in BH–magnetar binaries and provides an exact non-rotating baseline for extensions to the Kerr–Bertotti–Robinson case.

papers.title: Optimal measurement-based quantum thermal machines in a finite-size system
summary_bullets: The paper proposes a measurement-based quantum thermal machine whose working medium is a finite-size array of coupled two-level systems with Ising-like interactions and extracts work from measurement back-action via a three-stroke cycle (measurement, feedback, thermalization). It derives analytic stationarity conditions (Theorem 1) for optimal local feedback angles that minimize the post-feedback energy and presents two numerical algorithms (hybrid local–global search and grid-sweep) to obtain global optima for small-to-moderate system sizes. Numerical results show efficiency peaks in the projective-measurement limit, that symmetry breaking (detuning or weak coupling) enlarges exploitable energy gaps, and that performance is robust to ~10° feedback-pulse errors; the framework is platform-agnostic and implementable on superconducting, trapped-ion, or NMR setups.

Why Yanbei would care: the work directly addresses measurement back-action, optimal feedback control, and the thermodynamic cost of measurement/erasure—areas closely tied to Yanbei's expertise in quantum measurement theory, back-action evasion, and optomechanical control. The analytic stationarity conditions and scalable optimization strategies could inform design of feedback and control protocols in optomechanical or spin-based experiments he cares about, and the emphasis on experimental implementability and robustness is relevant for proposals that seek observable signatures of nonclassicality (and, by extension, for designing sensitive schemes to probe gravity-induced decoherence/entanglement in tabletop platforms).

Keywords/topics: measurement-based quantum thermal machines, quantum thermodynamics, measurement back-action, feedback control, finite-size coupled two-level systems, Ising-like interaction, optimization algorithms (hybrid local–global, grid sweep), projective-measurement limit, symmetry-breaking enhancements, robustness to feedback errors; Published on arXiv 2026-03-26 (very recent).

papers.title: Binary neutron star mergers with a subsolar mass star
summary_bullets: The authors present fully general‑relativistic numerical‑relativity simulations of unequal‑mass binary neutron star mergers that include a subsolar‑mass component (they study configurations like 1.8 M⊙ + 0.7 M⊙), yielding tidal deformabilities for the light star up to O(10^4).

They find mass transfer from the light star, substantially lower disruption/merger frequencies (∼990–1050 Hz) than in equal‑mass cases, and a large increase in dynamically ejected mass relative to existing phenomenological fits. The paper hybridizes NR and analytical inspirals to show that while current waveform models miss the late disruption physics, mismatches remain ≲7×10^−4 and parameter‑estimation injections at LIGO–Virgo design sensitivity show no significant biases for realistic SNRs (≲100).
No extra technical note provided.

papers.title: Particle motions and gravitational waveforms in rotating black hole spacetimes of loop quantum gravity
summary_bullets: The paper builds two Kerr‑like rotating black‑hole spacetimes by applying a revised Newman–Janis algorithm to spherically symmetric loop‑quantum‑gravity (LQG) seed metrics and introduces a holonomy regularization parameter ξ that encodes quantum corrections and reduces to Kerr when ξ→0. The authors analyze horizon structure (including extremal conditions and constraints on ξ and a), derive integrable timelike geodesics with a Carter constant, and map how ξ shifts orbital quantities (LMBO, LISCO, EISCO, rISCO) and confines off‑equatorial motion toward the equator. Using a simplified extreme‑mass‑ratio inspiral (EMRI) model at leading‑order post‑Newtonian order they compute gravitational waveforms from periodic prograde orbits and show that increasing ξ enhances waveform deviations near the horizon, highlighting potentially observable LQG imprints for future space detectors.

Why Yanbei would care: the paper directly connects an explicit family of effective, rotating LQG metrics to geodesic structure and EMRI waveforms—areas Yanbei has worked on (EMRIs, black‑hole perturbation theory, ISCO/MBO analyses and waveform modeling) and is interested in (strong‑gravity tests tied to observables, event‑horizon physics). Although the waveform treatment is a leading‑order PN toy model rather than a full perturbative/Teukolsky/self‑force calculation, the demonstrated shifts in ISCO/MBO, Carter‑constant confinement, and enhanced near‑horizon waveform signatures provide concrete targets for more rigorous follow‑ups (self‑force, Teukolsky QNM/waveform calculations or parameter‑estimation impact studies for LISA/Taiji/TianQin).

Keywords/topics: loop quantum gravity, holonomy corrections (ξ), revised Newman–Janis algorithm, rotating regular black holes, horizon/extremality conditions, Carter constant, timelike geodesics (ISCO/MBO, precessing/periodic orbits), EMRIs, gravitational waveforms (leading‑order PN), observational prospects (LISA/Taiji/TianQin); arXiv preprint posted 26 March 2026 (very recent).

papers.title: Explicit States with Two-sided Long-Range Magic
summary_bullets: This paper constructs explicit many-body states that provably possess two-sided long-range magic—i.e., nonstabilizerness that cannot be produced by either Clifford◦FDU or FDU◦Clifford circuits—thereby placing them outside the first level of the recently proposed magic hierarchy. Concrete examples include the "ZX-cat" state (|ψ> ∝ |0^n> + |+^n>) and ground states of certain nonabelian quantum-double/string-net topological orders, with rigorous proofs that preparation by those circuit classes is impossible up to constant or inverse-polynomial error. The work introduces new proof techniques tying long-range magic to many-body phase structure and principles of quantum error correction, connecting circuit complexity, topological order, and nonstabilizerness.

Why you would care: the paper identifies which macroscopic many-body states carry robust, topologically protected "magic" resources and gives constructive examples and lower bounds, which directly bears on resource accounting for preparing and certifying nonclassical macroscopic states (relevant to macroscopic quantum mechanics and optomechanical platforms you study). Its link between nonabelian topological order, error-correcting structure, and provable hardness of state preparation informs both fault-tolerant encoding strategies and criteria for experimentally distinguishing genuinely nonstabilizer quantum resources—topics that touch your interests in quantum error correction, macroscopic quantum states, and foundations that underlie proposals to probe gravity-induced decoherence/entanglement.

Keywords/topics: long-range magic; nonstabilizerness/magic; magic hierarchy; ZX‑cat (magical cat) state; nonabelian topological order; quantum error correction; circuit complexity; many-body phases. Published on arXiv as quant-ph:2603.25023 (v1) 26 Mar 2026.

papers.title: Resource-optimal quantum mode parameter estimation with multimode Gaussian states
summary_bullets: This paper develops a resource‑normalized framework for quantum mode‑parameter estimation with multimode pure Gaussian states, identifying the signal photon number N_S and two modal resources — the generator mean ḡ and its variance Δg — as the physically meaningful quantities that govern precision. The authors derive a tight upper bound on the quantum Fisher information F ≤ (8 ḡ^2 + 4 Δg^2) N_S^2 + O(N_S), construct analytically optimal Gaussian probes (two‑mode squeezers in the generator eigenbasis) that saturate Heisenberg scaling, and prove multimode homodyne detection is an optimal measurement. They also show ḡ and Δg play distinct operational roles (phase‑sensitive vs phase‑insensitive) and apply the framework to time/frequency shifts and spatial beam-parameter estimation (displacement and tilt).

Why Yanbei would care: the paper gives a clear, operational resource accounting and an end‑to‑end optimal protocol (probe + measurement) for mode-parameter tasks that underlie interferometry and optomechanical sensing — areas central to Yanbei's work on quantum noise, quantum measurement theory, back‑action evasion and optomechanics. The identification of generator-eigenmode basis as the natural basis for optimal probes, the analytic construction of two‑mode squeezed optimal states, and the phase‑sensitive vs phase‑insensitive distinction directly inform design choices for quantum‑limited readout in gravitational‑wave–style interferometers, quantum-limited lidar/radar and tabletop optomechanical tests probing decoherence or gravity-induced effects. Finally, the result that multimode homodyne attains the bound is practically useful for experimental proposals that Yanbei and collaborators pursue, since it ties optimal metrology to realizable detection schemes.

Keywords/topics: multimode Gaussian states, quantum Fisher information, resource‑normalized quantum metrology, Heisenberg limit, two‑mode squeezing, multimode homodyne detection, time-delay and frequency-shift estimation, beam displacement/tilt, mode engineering, optomechanics. papers.title: Dynamical Tidal Response of Regular Black Holes: Perturbative Analysis and Shell EFT Interpretation
summary_bullets: We compute frequency-dependent (dynamical) tidal Love numbers for three regular black-hole geometries (Bardeen, Hayward, Fan–Wang) by solving the coupled linear perturbation equations with ingoing-wave/asymptotic boundary conditions and by constructing a shell EFT that encodes renormalized, frequency-dependent response functions. In the polar sector the dynamical TLNs show strong dispersion with oscillatory and resonant features that smoothly reproduce the static limit, while in the axial sector gravitational and electromagnetic perturbations remain coupled and exhibit a pronounced frequency-dependent enhancement near extremality. The shell-EFT analysis isolates scheme-independent logarithmic running from scheme-dependent finite parts, provides gauge-invariant Wilson coefficients matched to perturbation theory, and thus establishes dynamical TLNs as well-defined EFT observables that probe near-horizon and interior structure absent in the static limit.

Yanbei would care because this paper sits at the intersection of black-hole perturbation theory, tidal interactions, and EFT parameterizations—areas where he has contributed to waveform modelling, EMRI/strong-field calculations, and tests of GR; the frequency-dependent (and resonant) tidal responses reported here provide concrete finite-size corrections that can accumulate in inspiral phasing or produce distinctive signatures for EMRIs and high-precision tests. Moreover, the shell-EFT matching to renormalized Wilson coefficients gives a clean, gauge-invariant template for incorporating these effects into waveform models and for separating measurable running from model-dependent finite pieces when constraining modified interiors or deviations from vacuum GR.

Keywords/topics: dynamical tidal Love numbers (frequency-dependent TLNs), regular/non-singular black holes (Bardeen, Hayward, Fan–Wang), black-hole perturbation theory, polar and axial sectors, shell EFT / renormalized response, resonances and dispersion, near-horizon/interior probes, potential GW/EMRI waveform imprints. Published on arXiv 25 March 2026 (very recent).

papers.title: Topological signatures in Kerr-Sen AdS black hole thermodynamics
summary_bullets: The authors apply Duan’s ϕ‑mapping topological current theory together with a novel complex‑residue method to classify the thermodynamic phase structure of the Kerr‑Sen AdS black hole (a rotating, dilaton‑charged solution from heterotic string theory). They find three on‑shell branches (small, intermediate, large) carrying winding numbers +1, −1, +1 respectively, giving a total topological charge W=+1, and show that this topological class is invariant under variations of the dilaton charge while being controlled by the rotation parameter. They also analytically continue the off‑shell Gibbs free energy into the complex plane and demonstrate that isolated singularities of the characterized function have residues that directly encode the winding numbers, providing an alternative residue‑based classification of critical points.

Why Yanbei would care: The paper develops coordinate‑independent, topological diagnostics of black‑hole phase structure and introduces a residue technique that codifies critical‑point creation/annihilation—methods that complement Yanbei’s interests in black‑hole structure and universal, observable signatures of strong gravity. Although the work is thermodynamic/AdS‑focused rather than waveform modelling, the emphasis on rotation‑driven changes in global topology and on invariant quantities under dilaton deformations may map onto questions Yanbei studies (how spin and additional fields modify stability, quasinormal spectra, nonlinear oscillations, and holographic observables), and the residue/topological viewpoint could inspire new invariant diagnostics for perturbation theory or ringdown analyses. In short, it offers conceptual tools that could bridge thermodynamic topology and dynamical/observable properties of black holes—relevant to testing gravity and to holographic interpretations Yanbei follows.

Keywords/topics: Kerr‑Sen AdS, thermodynamic topology, Duan’s ϕ‑mapping, complex residue method, winding numbers, dilaton charge, rotation effects, phase transitions, holography. arXiv:2603.24686 (submitted 25 Mar 2026; PDF dated 27 Mar 2026) — very recent.

papers.title: Fractal universe and quantum gravity made simple
summary_bullets: The authors construct a covariant, self-adjoint "fractional quantum gravity" (FQG) by formulating QFT on multiscale/fractal spacetimes with a nonlocal kinetic form factor F(□) that encodes dimensional flow and a fractional UV scaling γ. They prove perturbative super-renormalizability (power-counting leads to finiteness for γ>2, one-loop super-renormalizability for larger γ) and argue perturbative unitarity by expressing the propagator as the standard graviton pole plus a continuum of complex-conjugate modes that are removed from the physical spectrum via the fakeon prescription. The paper works out the propagator and gravitational action, and discusses phenomenology: standard GW and solar-system tests are largely insensitive if the fundamental scale ℓ* is Planckian, but short-distance modifications to Newtonian potential, microscopic regular black holes, and possible deviations in black-hole shadows and waveforms at scales ∼ℓ* are identified as targets for future observational study.

Yanbei would care because the paper gives a concrete, top-down quantum-gravity proposal that connects formal field-theory ingredients (fractional/nonlocal kinetic operators, spectral-dimension running, propagator structure and fakeon projection) to potential strong-gravity observables he studies: black-hole structure, waveforms, tails and tests of GR. The construction touches his prior work and interests in black-hole perturbation theory, waveform modeling and strong-gravity tests (including how fundamental high-energy modifications imprint on luminosity distances, GW dispersion and quasinormal spectra), and it proposes calculable short-scale modifications and candidate regular black holes whose QNM/shadow/waveform signatures could be modeled and constrained with GW and EM data.

keywords/topics: fractional quantum gravity, fractal/multiscale spacetime, spectral dimension, nonlocal kinetic operator F(□), propagator with continuum modes, renormalizability (super-renormalizable for γ>2), perturbative unitarity and fakeon prescription, black-hole modifications, gravitational-wave phenomenology, modified Newtonian potential. Published: arXiv:2603.24593 (hep-th), 25 March 2026 — very recent.

papers.title: Quasinormal Modes of a Massive Scalar Field in 4D Einstein--Gauss--Bonnet Black Hole Spacetimes
summary_bullets: The paper computes quasinormal-mode spectra, grey-body factors, and absorption cross-sections for a massive scalar field on the asymptotically-flat four-dimensional Einstein–Gauss–Bonnet (4D-EGB) black-hole background while restricting the Gauss–Bonnet coupling to a stability-admissible window. Using high-order WKB–Padé approximants (up to WKB16) and independent time-domain characteristic integration with Prony fits, the author cross-validates frequency- and time-domain results and reports robust convergence where a single-barrier potential peak exists. The main results show that increasing the scalar mass strongly reduces damping and drives modes toward long-lived quasi-resonant behavior, whereas the Gauss–Bonnet coupling produces only mild deformations of the spectrum and scattering (consistent with an effective-potential barrier interpretation), and the paper provides tables/plots that quantify these trends for multiple ℓ and α within the stable region.

Why Yanbei would care: this work sits squarely at the intersection of black-hole perturbation theory, ringdown spectroscopy, and beyond-GR tests — areas Yanbei has long worked on — because it provides precision QNM and scattering diagnostics for a higher-curvature deformation (EGB) that can be used as a baseline for strong-field tests. The explicit WKB16 vs time-domain cross-checks and the mapping of how a finite scalar mass produces quasi-resonances and modifies grey-body/absorption behaviour are directly relevant to waveform-systematics, parameter estimation for non-GR signatures or ultralight dark-matter fields, and to interpreting ringdown/late-time observables in future high-SNR detectors.

Keywords/topics: quasinormal modes, 4D Einstein–Gauss–Bonnet (EGB) gravity, massive scalar fields, quasi-resonances, WKB–Padé (high-order WKB16), time-domain characteristic integration, grey-body factors, absorption cross-section, ringdown spectroscopy. papers.title: Robust Parametric Quantum Gate Against Stochastic Time-Varying Noise
summary_bullets: This paper extends the quantum control robustness landscape (QCRL) to realistic stochastic, time‑varying noise by integrating the filter‑function formalism and names the resulting method FF‑QCRL. The algorithm minimizes a generalized robustness metric that maps control parameters and noise power spectra to gate infidelity, enabling generation of parametric gate families via RIPV while preserving both fidelity and spectral robustness. As an auxiliary route the authors introduce a variational quantum circuit (VQC) surrogate to approximate the robustness metric and its gradients, and validate FF‑QCRL numerically for a single‑qubit Rx(θ) under multi‑band detuning noise with filter‑function analysis and Monte‑Carlo tests.

Why Yanbei would care: FF‑QCRL directly addresses spectrally structured, time‑dependent noise using filter functions, which is the same formalism used to connect control pulses to observable decoherence in precision experiments; that makes it relevant to designing noise‑resilient controls for optomechanical and interferometric platforms he works on and for separating technical noise from subtle effects like gravity‑induced decoherence or entanglement. The introduction of a VQC surrogate ties to his interests in quantum learning/variational methods and could be useful for low‑overhead, experiment‑driven optimization; moreover, robust parametric gates (and the RIPV traversal of robustness level sets) can improve experimental protocols and metrological readouts where maintaining both a tunable operation and noise immunity is critical.

Keywords/topics: filter function, QCRL, FF‑QCRL, robust quantum control, parametric quantum gates, RIPV, stochastic time‑varying noise, VQC surrogate, NISQ; published on arXiv 25 Mar 2026 (very recent).

papers.title: Correlated Atom Loss as a Resource for Quantum Error Correction
summary_bullets: The paper introduces a decoder for the rotated surface code that exploits correlated atom-loss events in neutral-atom processors by constructing a loss graph and dynamically updating posterior loss probabilities to effectively convert delayed erasure channels into standard erasure channels.

Leveraging teleportation-based loss-detection units and an approximate but highly parallelizable inference algorithm, the decoder is compatible with real-time operation and yields up to an order-of-magnitude reduction in logical error probability while increasing the loss threshold from 3.2% to 4% in the fully correlated model.

The authors model circuit-level, partially correlated loss stemming from Rydberg-mediated two-qubit gates, present both an accurate (non-scalable) and a fast scalable correlated-loss decoder, and benchmark robust gains across experimentally relevant regimes.

papers.title: A Longitudinal Analysis of the CEC Single-Objective Competitions (2010-2024) and Implications for Variational Quantum Optimization
summary_bullets: The paper presents a longitudinal analysis of IEEE CEC Single‑Objective Optimization competitions from 2010–2024, tracing how benchmark changes shaped winning algorithmic mechanisms. It identifies the 2014 introduction of dense rotation matrices as a turning point that rendered benchmarks non‑separable and favoured rotationally invariant Differential Evolution variants (notably L‑SHADE), and documents a post‑2020 trend toward high‑complexity hybrid optimizers combining mechanisms like Eigenvector Crossover and reinforcement learning. As a novel contribution it argues that structural similarities between modern CEC benchmarks and Variational Quantum Algorithm landscapes (non‑separability, correlated parameters, sampling noise) make these evolved CEC solvers promising candidates for improving variational quantum optimization and quantum control.

Yanbei should care because the paper directly addresses classical optimizer strategies for correlated, noisy, and non‑separable parameter landscapes—precisely the problems that arise in VQAs and quantum control tasks relevant to his work on quantum measurement, optomechanics, and macroscopic quantum systems. Rotationally invariant DE variants (L‑SHADE family) and population‑based techniques flagged here map onto concrete fixes for entanglement‑induced parameter correlations and finite‑sampling noise (population averaging, ensemble strategies), which could improve variational circuit training for quantum sensing, control of mechanical degrees of freedom, or protocols probing gravity‑induced decoherence/entanglement.

Keywords/topics: Global optimization, Evolutionary algorithms, Differential Evolution, L‑SHADE, rotational invariance, non‑separability, hybrid optimizers, Variational Quantum Algorithms, quantum control; Published on arXiv: 25 Mar 2026 (very recent).

papers.title: Data-driven synthesis of high-fidelity triaxial magnetic waveforms for quantum control
summary_bullets: The paper presents a system for generating arbitrary, triaxial magnetic waveforms with spectral content spanning from DC to tens of kHz, targeted at quantum control and spin manipulation. It uses a data-driven compensation of amplifier–coil dynamics by identifying a Finite Impulse Response (FIR) model via weighted least squares on measured input–output data and then computes precompensated driving voltages through Wiener-regularized frequency-domain inversion. Experimental validation on representative spin-manipulation waveforms demonstrates high fidelity, sharp transient performance, synchronous three-axis control, and rapid reconfiguration without relying on detailed circuit models.

This is directly relevant to Yanbei’s interests because high-fidelity, synchronized multi-axis magnetic control mitigates classical noise sources and control errors that otherwise limit coherent spin/atomic protocols and precision measurements—areas tied to his work on quantum measurement theory, quantum noise in interferometers, back-action evasion, and optomechanical control. Moreover, the method’s ability to suppress switching transients and precisely shape control fields is valuable for tabletop tests of gravity-induced decoherence/entanglement and other quantum-gravity–adjacent experiments where spurious magnetic couplings must be tightly controlled, and for implementing robust control layers useful in quantum learning / error-mitigation experiments.

Keywords/topics: triaxial magnetic waveform generation; FIR/WLS system identification; frequency-domain inversion (Wiener regularisation); feed-forward precompensation; spin manipulation; atomic quantum control; DAC-driven three-axis amplifiers; DC–tens of kHz bandwidth; experimental validation. papers.title: Efficient Many-Body Shadow Metrology via Clifford Lensing
summary_bullets: Using experimentally accessible Clifford operations acting jointly on states and observables, the authors introduce "Clifford lensing"—θ-independent Clifford isometries that coherently refocus distributed many-body phase information onto a reduced subsystem while preserving the quantum Fisher information and expectation values. They prove constructive results linking [n,1] stabilizer codes to deterministic Clifford phase kickback, characterize when full localization to one qubit (stabilizer-compatible) versus partial localization to k qubits (stabilizer-incompatible) is possible, and show that Clifford lensing reduces the Pauli weight of the symmetric logarithmic derivative (SLD) to enable efficient classical-shadow estimation. The framework is implemented experimentally with liquid-state NMR up to 15 qubits, introducing metrologically sufficient channels and partial shadow tomography under restricted collective control and demonstrating near–Heisenberg-limited phase sensitivity with greatly reduced measurement complexity.

Why Yanbei would care: this work directly addresses the gap between formal optimal measurements (SLD eigenbases) and experimentally accessible measurements in many-body interferometry—a central theme in his work on quantum measurement theory, quantum noise in interferometers, back-action evasion, and optomechanics. The explicit mapping between error-correcting codes and interferometric phase kickback (Clifford lensing) provides a toolbox for concentrating weak, distributed phase signals onto few degrees of freedom, which is valuable for designing feasible Heisenberg-scaling probes and readouts in constrained platforms (including proposals for tabletop probes of gravity-induced phases or decoherence). Moreover, the metrologically sufficient channels and shadow protocols show how quantum-information methods (Clifford circuits, shadows, stabilizer techniques) can be used to reduce measurement complexity in realistic experiments—aligning with his interests in combining QIS techniques with precision sensing and foundational tests.

Keywords/topics: Clifford lensing, symmetric logarithmic derivative (SLD), quantum Fisher information, stabilizer codes / phase kickback, Pauli-weight reduction, classical shadows / partial shadow tomography, metrologically sufficient channels, collective Clifford control, NMR demonstration (≤15 qubits). Published: arXiv v1 25 Mar 2026 (very recent).

papers.title: Identical Quantum Particles as Potential Parts
summary_bullets: The paper reconstructs the quantum symmetrization procedure (the Feynman quantum symmetrization algorithm, fQSA) from an operational indistinguishability postulate plus an isolation condition and shows that the distinctive behaviour of identical particles derives from the conjunction of identicality and the active nature of quantum measurements. Using this reconstruction the author formulates two complementary object‑models—persistence and non‑persistence—built from detection events rather than particle labels, and proposes a metaphysics of 'potential parts' in which identical particles are actual parts only when sufficiently isolated but otherwise exist only potentially, yielding restricted transtemporal identity and an in‑principle loss of reidentifiability. The novelty is methodological and interpretational: by shifting focus from the standard algebraic symmetrization rules to an operationally grounded framework the work exposes implicit experimental heuristics (e.g. no‑overlap rules) and reframes identical‑particle behaviour as measurement‑contextual, with direct consequences for how models connect to observed detection data.

You would care because this paper directly interrogates the operational assumptions that underwrite how detection events are modeled as persistent particles — precisely the sort of measurement‑theory issues you and collaborators use when analysing quantum noise, back‑action, QND strategies and macroscopic/optomechanical experiments; surfacing hidden heuristics about labeling and reidentifiability can change how one interprets interferometric or matter‑wave readouts and thus affect claims about observed decoherence or entanglement (including proposals to detect gravity‑induced effects). The reconstruction approach also supplies a concrete operational language for designing tests and for assessing when particle‑based descriptions break down, which is useful when planning or interpreting tabletop probes of foundational phenomena (e.g. gravity‑induced decoherence/entanglement) where assumptions about identity and measurement matter.

Keywords/topics: identical quantum particles, symmetrization/fQSA, operational indistinguishability, reconstruction, measurement theory, persistence vs non‑persistence, potential parts, transtemporal identity, experimental assumptions; arXiv:2603.23813 — posted/published March 25–26, 2026 (very recent).

papers.title: Precision bounds for frequency estimation under collective dephasing and open-loop control
summary_bullets: They derive rigorous, state-independent precision bounds for entanglement-assisted frequency estimation under classical collective dephasing with arbitrary temporal correlations, showing that the achievable scaling is set solely by the short‑time behavior of the decoherence function. For Markovian (white) dephasing the precision is limited by a probe‑independent constant (precluding super‑classical scaling), while for stationary temporally correlated noise classical states can approach the noiseless limit and asymptotic quantum advantage is precluded. They construct generalized Ramsey protocols (GHZ and one‑axis‑twisted/squeezed inputs with pre‑readout disentangling and perfect‑echo) that saturate the bounds and prove two no‑go theorems that collective open‑loop control cannot restore super‑classical scaling, although constant‑factor gains are possible for colored noise.

Yanbei would care because the paper addresses fundamental decoherence limits and achievable protocols in a noise model (collective dephasing) directly relevant to precision interferometry and macroscopic quantum sensors—areas where his prior work on quantum noise, quantum measurement theory, back‑action evasion, and optomechanics is central. The constructive saturation of the bounds with experimentally realistic squeezed states and echo/disentangling operations, together with rigorous no‑go results for open‑loop control, provides concrete benchmarks and constraints that inform when entanglement, dynamical control, or error‑correction‑inspired strategies can meaningfully improve (or cannot improve) metrological performance—making it directly applicable to his interest in experimental signatures of decoherence and quantum‑limited sensing.

Keywords/topics: collective dephasing, frequency estimation, quantum metrology, quantum Fisher information, Ramsey interferometry, GHZ states, spin squeezing, one‑axis twisting, perfect echo, open‑loop control, dynamical decoupling, no‑go theorems, Markovian vs colored noise, standard quantum limit vs Heisenberg scaling. Published on 2026-03-25 (arXiv:2603.23804), ~1.6 days old.

papers.title: Testing Dark Energy with Black Hole Ringdown
summary_bullets: The paper demonstrates that a recently discovered stable, time-dependent cubic Galileon black‑hole solution carries cosmological ‘hair’ and that this hair parametrically links the cosmological scalar dynamics to the short‑range black hole background used for perturbation theory.  Perturbations around this quasi‑stationary background (worked out in Regge–Wheeler gauge) yield decoupled modified Regge–Wheeler and Zerilli equations and an analytic/numerical result that QNM frequencies are rescaled as ω = √β · ωGR, with β fitted numerically to the cosmological scalar velocity by β = 11.82 (q/q0 − 1) + 1.  The rescaling produces O(10–40%) shifts in ringdown frequencies (a smoking‑gun signature), the effect is degenerate with a mass rescaling (so inspiral/merger mass information is needed to break the degeneracy), and Fisher forecasts give σ(q/q0) ∼ 10−2 for LVK and ∼10−4 for LISA under their assumptions.

Yanbei should care because this paper directly connects cosmological dynamical dark energy to concrete, strong‑field observables (QNM spectra) using black‑hole perturbation theory and waveform forecasting — areas central to his past work on black‑hole perturbations, QNMs, waveform modeling and tests of GR.  The result offers a calculable, modelled target (the √β frequency shift and the explicit β(q/q0) relation) that can be incorporated into ringdown/inspiral joint analyses or waveform templates, and it naturally raises extensions Yanbei is well‑placed to pursue (spinning remnants, nonlinear mode coupling, EMRI probes, and refined merger modelling to lift the mass–β degeneracy).

papers.title: Reducing cosmological degeneracies by combining multiple classes of LISA gravitational-wave standard sirens
summary_bullets: This paper presents the first joint LISA cosmological inference that combines EMRI dark sirens (z≲1) and MBHB bright sirens (z≳1) within a hierarchical Bayesian framework using simulated catalogs, Fisher-matrix errors, and galaxy cross-matching. It demonstrates that the complementary redshift leverage of low-z EMRIs and high-z MBHBs partially breaks degeneracies among late-time parameters, yielding percent-level constraints on H0 (≈0.6–2% depending on mission duration and assumptions) and competitive bounds on w0 (few–10%). The novelty is the quantitative demonstration that combining dark and bright LISA sirens—with selection effects and realistic counterpart scenarios—rotates degeneracy directions in the h–Ωm and w0 parameter space and substantially tightens late-time cosmological inferences.

You would care because the study directly involves EMRIs (a system you have worked on) and addresses how waveform/systematic assumptions, population modeling, and selection effects propagate into observable cosmological parameters; these are areas where your expertise in black‑hole perturbation theory, EMRI modeling, and statistical GW inference (template banks, hierarchical methods, tests of GR) can both critique and extend the analysis. The paper also links strong‑gravity observables to astrophysical populations and data‑analysis strategies that matter for LISA-era probes of the event horizon, nonlinear BH dynamics, and independent late‑time cosmology—topics that align tightly with your current interests in strong‑gravity tests and EMRI science.

papers.title: Enhanced Dark Matter Quantum Sensing via Geometric Phase
summary_bullets: The paper proposes a geometric-phase-based quantum sensing protocol for dispersively coupled qubit–oscillator (transmon–cavity) systems that maps a weak dark-matter-induced cavity drive onto an amplified, spin-dependent geometric phase by combining large coherent displacements with squeezing and a spin-echo sequence. They show this converts the weak displacement into an enhanced quantum Fisher information scaling ∝β^2 (β = α e^r), surpassing the standard quantum limit while accounting for cavity loss and qubit decoherence, and projecting up to 1–2 orders of magnitude improved sensitivity to axion and dark-photon searches for realistic device parameters. The analysis uses Magnus-expansion-derived evolution operators, explicit QFI calculations, and decoherence trade-off estimates (cavity-induced which-path dephasing and spin-echo limits), and gives experimental benchmarks (squeezing ≲15 dB, β∼20, T_c1 choices) and extensions to entangled-cavity and more general control protocols.

You would care because the protocol sits squarely at the intersection of quantum measurement theory and practical macroscopic quantum sensing—areas where you and collaborators have deep contributions (quantum noise in interferometers, QND/back‑action evasion, quantum trajectories and optomechanics). The work offers a concrete way to amplify a weak, physically motivated signal into a measurable geometric phase while explicitly treating measurement back-action and decoherence, making it directly relevant to designing tabletop searches for new physics (dark matter, and by analogy methods for gravity‑induced decoherence/entanglement) and to applying squeezing/active control techniques you study to improve sensitivity of macroscopic quantum devices.

papers.title: Energy-Morawetz estimates for Teukolsky equations in perturbations of Kerr
summary_bullets: In this paper Ma and Szeftel prove global energy and Morawetz (integrated local energy decay) estimates for solutions of the tensorial Teukolsky equations on spacetimes whose metrics are perturbations (compatible with nonlinear applications) of subextremal Kerr for the full |a|<m range. They formulate the Teukolsky system as a coupled wave/transport hierarchy in a non‑integrable tensorial formalism, introduce a regular scalarization procedure to reduce tensorial waves to well‑behaved scalar systems, and use r‑foliation–adapted microlocal multipliers (extending scalar‑wave techniques) together with controls of linear coupling terms to derive the estimates. The result supplies the key linear energy‑Morawetz bounds needed to remove the slow‑rotation restriction in current Kerr stability proofs and is presented as an essential step toward a full nonlinear stability theorem for all subextremal Kerr metrics.

Why Yanbei would care: the paper delivers rigorous decay and integrated energy control for the radiative curvature variables (Teukolsky fields) that directly underpin late‑time tails, quasinormal‑mode decay rates, and nonlinear coupling effects important for ringdown and EMRI waveform modeling—areas tied to his prior work on black‑hole perturbation theory, waveform templates and gravitational‑wave tails. The introduced regular scalarization and microlocal multiplier machinery provide practical tools to convert tensorial perturbations into scalar estimates useful for extracting observables and for controlling nonlinear error terms in stability analyses, aligning with his interest in strong‑gravity tests and nonlinear black‑hole dynamics. By treating the full subextremal range rather than only slow rotation, the work strengthens the theoretical basis for using Kerr ringdown and late‑time signals to probe horizon physics and potential deviations from GR.

Keywords/topics: Teukolsky equations, energy and Morawetz (EMF) estimates, tensorial non‑integrable formalism, regular scalarization, r‑foliation microlocal multipliers, Teukolsky wave/transport system, Kerr stability conjecture, subextremal Kerr (|a|<m). papers.title: Encoded Quantum Signal Processing for Heisenberg-Limited Metrology
summary_bullets: The paper introduces Encoded Quantum Signal Processing (EQSP), a framework that unifies quantum error detection and quantum signal processing to map multi-qubit metrology into an effective single-qubit problem where syndrome measurements implement nonlinear signal transformations. Using repetition codes and syndrome-conditioned measurements (without active recovery) the authors prove one can restore Heisenberg-limited scaling under realistic noise, show that product-state sensing with only syndrome post-processing is fundamentally SQL-limited, and present four protocols (entanglement-based, sequential amplification, concatenated, and an adaptive GHZ binary search) that achieve Heisenberg precision with exponential error suppression in code distance. They further analyze spatially inhomogeneous fields via Bayesian marginalization, give quantum Fisher information bounds and device-quality conditions for preserved scaling, and validate finite-size behavior and practical thresholds with numerical simulations.

This is directly relevant to Yanbei's interests because EQSP provides a concrete, noise-robust route to preserve entanglement-enhanced (Heisenberg) sensitivity in realistic detectors—connecting to his work on quantum measurement theory, quantum noise in interferometers, QND/back-action evasion, and optomechanics—and it frames error-detection/syndrome readout as an active signal-processing primitive that could be adapted to interferometric or optomechanical sensor architectures. EQSP also sits at the intersection of quantum error correction, quantum signal processing, and metrology, matching Yanbei's current interest in quantum learning and QEC methods for preserving quantum resources, and it could enable more robust tabletop tests for gravity-induced decoherence or gravity-mediated entanglement by extending Heisenberg scaling into noisy regimes.
